sourceMovie.title: Red Lights
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Red Lights works as a thriller movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The film leans hard into its core genre, which is exactly what fans say they wanted more of from blockbusters in that era.

recommendations.title: Source Code
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Source Code (2011) is the kind of film Red Lights fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The thriller overlap is real, but it's the tense emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: The Game
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Game shares Red Lights's gift for thriller — the 1997 film delivers drama without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Enemy
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Red Lights compelling — the thriller, the mystery — shows up in Enemy (2014) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that tense storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Vertigo
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The thriller energy of Red Lights is alive in Vertigo (1958), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a tense payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Bad Times at the El Royale
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Bad Times at the El Royale (2018) operates in the same thriller as Red Lights, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The dark payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: The Mechanic
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Mechanic (2011) belongs to the same conversation as Red Lights — both anchor their thriller in action emotion that doesn't let go. The dark undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: The Number 23
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Red Lights's thriller stayed with you, The Number 23 (2007) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The mystery through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Paprika
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Red Lights excels in thriller, Paprika (2006) doubles down on science-fiction to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Pi
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Pi (1998) is the natural next step after Red Lights: same thriller DNA, different perspective. The tense streak and mystery elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Solace
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Red Lights who respond to its thriller will find Solace (2015) immediately familiar. Both films share a crime sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

faq.question: What should I watch after Red Lights?
faq.answer: Try Source Code, The Game, Enemy—each shares DNA with Red Lights while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Red Lights?
faq.answer: Red Lights (2012) is best described as Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Red Lights worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 6.2/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this thriller lane.
